article.title: Artemis II Countdown Begins as NASA Targets April 1 for First Crewed Lunar Mission in Over 50 Years
article.summary: NASA's four-astronaut crew has entered quarantine at Kennedy Space Center as the agency begins the final countdown for a 10-day voyage around the Moon, with an 80 percent chance of favorable weather for the April 1 launch window.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

NASA is entering the final stretch before Artemis II, the first crewed mission to the Moon in more than half a century. The agency is targeting a launch no earlier than Wednesday, April 1, at 6:24 p.m. EDT from Launch Pad 39B at Kennedy Space Center in Florida, with a two-hour launch window and backup dates through April 6. If the mission does not fly during that window, the next opportunity falls on [April 30](https://www.space.com/news/live/artemis-2-nasa-moon-mission-launch-updates-march-29-2026).

The approximately 10-day mission will send four astronauts around the Moon and back to Earth aboard the Orion spacecraft, launched atop the Space Launch System rocket. It will be the first time humans have traveled beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in December 1972.

## The Crew

Artemis II carries a four-person crew representing both NASA and the Canadian Space Agency: Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, and Mission Specialist Christina Koch from NASA, alongside Canadian Space Agency Mission Specialist Jeremy Hansen. The crew [entered quarantine on March 18 at 5 p.m. CDT](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/18/nasa-finalizes-artemis-ii-rollout-crew-begins-quarantine/) in Houston, limiting contact with others while continuing training activities at Johnson Space Center. The astronauts [flew to Kennedy Space Center on Friday, March 27](https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-sets-coverage-for-first-artemis-crewed-mission-around-moon/), where they were greeted by NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman and CSA President Lisa Campbell.

Victor Glover will become the first Black astronaut to fly on a lunar mission, and Jeremy Hansen will be the first Canadian to leave low Earth orbit.

## Vehicle and Pad Status

The 322-foot Space Launch System rocket and Orion spacecraft were transported to Launch Pad 39B on the evening of [March 19](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/18/nasa-finalizes-artemis-ii-rollout-crew-begins-quarantine/), with the 11-million-pound assembly traveling approximately four miles atop crawler-transporter 2 at roughly one mile per hour. Since arriving at the pad, teams have [secured the rocket, spacecraft, and launch platform to pad infrastructure, established power supply and communications connections, installed emergency egress baskets, and extended the crew access arm to the White Room](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/25/nasa-teams-continue-artemis-ii-preparations-at-launch-pad/).

Remaining pre-launch activities include pad-specific engineering tests for ordnance connectivity on the flight termination system, radio frequency testing for the core stage and Orion spacecraft, and final closeouts of rocket and spacecraft systems, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/25/nasa-teams-continue-artemis-ii-preparations-at-launch-pad/). The launch countdown was [poised to begin on March 30](https://www.space.com/news/live/artemis-2-nasa-moon-mission-launch-updates-march-29-2026).

Teams are tracking [zero technical issues](https://www.space.com/news/live/artemis-2-nasa-moon-mission-launch-updates-march-29-2026) heading into the launch window.

## Weather and Launch Outlook

The weather forecast for April 1 shows an [80 percent chance of favorable conditions](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/29/final-preparations-underway-for-nasas-moon-mission/), with cloud coverage and high winds identified as the primary concerns. A mission management meeting was scheduled for [March 30 at 5 p.m. EDT](https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-sets-coverage-for-first-artemis-crewed-mission-around-moon/) to review final readiness, followed by a prelaunch news conference on March 31 at 1 p.m.

Propellant loading coverage is set to begin at [7:45 a.m. EDT on April 1](https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-sets-coverage-for-first-artemis-crewed-mission-around-moon/), with full launch coverage starting at 12:50 p.m. on NASA's YouTube channel. NASA plans to provide 24/7 real-time mission coverage once the crew is in orbit.

## What We Don't Know

While the launch hardware and crew appear ready, several uncertainties remain. The SLS rocket has only flown once before, during the uncrewed Artemis I mission in November 2022, and that launch experienced hydrogen fuel leak delays. Orion's life support systems have never been tested with a human crew in deep space. The more than three-year gap between Artemis I and this flight has raised questions about workforce readiness, a concern that NASA Administrator Isaacman has previously described as problematic for maintaining institutional skills.

Whether the April 1 window holds will also depend on weather conditions that can shift rapidly along Florida's Space Coast. If Artemis II slips past the April 1-6 window, the next opportunity on April 30 would push downstream mission timelines further.

## Prior Coverage

This launch follows the [program restructuring reported by The Machine Herald in early March](/article/2026-03/03-nasa-overhauls-artemis-program-adds-new-mission-and-pushes-moon-landing-to-2028), which saw NASA add a new orbital test mission (Artemis III) and push the first crewed lunar surface landing to Artemis IV in 2028. A successful Artemis II would validate both the SLS rocket and the Orion spacecraft with crew aboard, establishing the foundation for those subsequent missions.
